topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope was thick, cream-colored, sealed with wax that felt cold and heavy in my palm. It arrived on a Tuesday, completely unannounced, a stark contrast to the usual junk mail I endured. I hadn't expected anything from Marcus, not in a decade. My jaw tightened as I slit the seal, the sound of the tearing paper echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. Inside, the script was formal, meticulously worded, an apology for something that felt ancient history to me. I read it once, twice, and then folded the paper with unnecessary force. I didn't put it in any filing system; I simply tucked it deep into the back of a seldom-used drawer, a silent monument to past foolishness. I stood up, pacing the length of the living room, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge of the coffee table. The air in the room felt suddenly too thin, charged with an unwelcome sense of obligation.

[story 2]

When the postman slid the thick parchment across the threshold, I didn't immediately reach for it. I watched him leave, my gaze fixed on the mailbox as if expecting the letter to retract. It was from Liam. Years. Years since the argument that had fractured everything between us, years since the sharp words had been exchanged. My breath hitched, a small, involuntary sound in the silence of my kitchen. I picked it up, my fingers tracing the embossed lettering. The words inside offered a complete retraction, a full acknowledgment of the damage done. A slow, tight smile pulled at the corner of my mouth, a gesture that didn't reach my eyes. I smoothed the paper flat on the counter, the crisp texture a deliberate counterpoint to the turmoil churning beneath my ribs. I didn't burn it. I just set it aside, a trophy of a conversation I had no intention of having.

[story 3]

I found the letter tucked inside a book I hadn't touched since the summer of the incident. It was a clumsy affair, the handwriting shaky, clearly written in haste. When I finally unfolded it, the sheer audacity of it—years later, someone finally admitting fault—made my stomach clench with a strange, hot energy. I reread the paragraphs, my eyes scanning the text with a detached scrutiny, searching for any hidden trick, any veiled demand. There was nothing but regret, thinly veiled. I let out a short, humorless laugh, shaking my head slightly. I crumpled the paper into a tight, hard ball, the creases sharp and unforgiving. I walked over to the window, looking out at the indifferent city skyline, feeling a powerful need to simply discard the weight of the sentiment entirely. It was finished.
